serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Awadhi Haara Kebab is approximately 120 g, comprising three to four pan-fried patties, as served in an Indian restaurant starter portion.

nutrient_source.title: Awadhi Haara Kebab — Singapore HPB Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Awadhi Haara Kebab (cr_id G00010794). Saturated fat not reported in the HPB record; estimated at 7.5 g/100g based on ICMR-NIN compositional data for cream- and cashew-enriched kebab preparations. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and several micronutrients not reported in the HPB record and set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Awadhi Haara Kebab. GI estimated at 28 (low) based on the dominant carbohydrate contributors: chickpeas (GI ~28, University of Sydney GI Database) and spinach (negligible carbohydrate contribution). The dish is very low in carbohydrate (5.24 g/100g), so glycemic load per 120 g serving = (28 × 5.24 × 1.20) / 100 = 1.75, rounded to 1.7. Chickpea GI anchor: Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 1080 mg/100g — far exceeds the 600 mg/100g upper threshold for hypertension-safe foods
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit Awadhi Haara Kebab due to its very high sodium content of 1080 mg/100g; if consumed, restrict portion to no more than 80 g and avoid additional salt-containing condiments or accompaniments at the same meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (5.24 g/100g) and low sugar (2.85 g/100g) are favourable, but high total fat (26.4 g/100g) and high saturated fat (~7.5 g/100g) may impair insulin sensitivity with regular consumption
type2_diabetes.guidance: Awadhi Haara Kebab has a low glycaemic load (GL ~1.7 per 120 g serving) and is unlikely to cause acute postprandial glucose spikes, but the high fat content from cream and cashew paste warrants portion control and monitoring of post-meal glucose, particularly in patients with insulin resistance.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (26.4 g/100g), estimated saturated fat (~7.5 g/100g), cholesterol (85 mg/100g), and very high sodium (1080 mg/100g) collectively elevate cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should treat Awadhi Haara Kebab as an occasional food only; the combination of high saturated fat, cholesterol, and sodium exceeds recommended limits for heart-healthy eating, and portion should be limited to 80–100 g with low-sodium accompaniments.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High saturated fat (~7.5 g/100g) exceeds the 5 g/100g RA threshold; high sodium (1080 mg/100g) worsens tissue inflammation; nightshade ingredients (green chillies) may aggravate nightshade-sensitive RA patients
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should limit or avoid Awadhi Haara Kebab due to high saturated fat and sodium; if consumed, request a reduced-cream preparation and confirm the absence of red meat; nightshade-sensitive patients should note the presence of green chillies.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Awadhi Haara Kebab is a vegetarian kebab originating from the Awadhi culinary tradition of Lucknow, Uttar Pradesh, India. The name 'Haara' (हारा) means 'green' in Hindi, reflecting the dish's vibrant green colour derived from fresh spinach and green chillies. The kebab is made from a spiced mixture of blanched spinach and boiled chickpeas, blended with cream and cashew paste to create a rich, smooth texture, then stuffed with paneer (fresh house cheese) and seasoned with dried fenugreek leaves (kasuri methi). The patties are shallow-fried or pan-grilled until golden on the outside while remaining soft and moist inside. The flavour profile is delicately spiced, mildly herbaceous, and creamy, with the fenugreek imparting a characteristic slightly bitter aromatic note. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides 302 kcal, 10.7 g protein, 26.4 g fat (of which approximately 7.5 g is saturated), 5.24 g carbohydrate, 2.85 g sugar, and 1080 mg sodium. The high sodium content makes it unsuitable for hypertension patients. The low carbohydrate and low glycaemic load (GL ~1.7 per 120 g serving) are favourable for glycaemic control, but the high saturated fat and cholesterol (85 mg/100g) raise cardiovascular concern. Dietary fibre is negligible at 0 g/100g. The dish is served as a starter or appetiser at Indian restaurants in Singapore and is popular at lunch and dinner. It is vegetarian, halal-friendly, and gluten-free, but contains dairy (paneer, cream) and tree nuts (cashew).

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010794, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat estimated from ICMR-NIN compositional data for comparable cream- and cashew-enriched kebab preparations. GI estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database chickpea anchor (GI 28), which is the dominant carbohydrate source in this very low-carbohydrate dish.
notes: dietary_fibre_g is reported as 0.0 g/100g in the HPB source; this is a confirmed zero per the HPB database entry, likely reflecting the processing of spinach and chickpeas into a smooth paste form that reduces measurable fibre. Saturated fat (7.5 g/100g) is not reported in the HPB record and has been estimated from ICMR-NIN data for cream- and cashew-enriched kebab preparations; this value should be verified against a laboratory analysis if available. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: these values are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for this dish, and no sufficiently close analogue entry in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central provides complete fatty acid and micronutrient profiles for this specific Awadhi preparation. The description mentions 'check peas' which is interpreted as 'chickpeas' (a typographical error in the source record). The dish is classified as vegetarian based on the ingredient description (spinach, chickpeas, paneer); however, some restaurant preparations may include minced meat — consumers should confirm with the restaurant. Halal status is assumed based on vegetarian ingredients but should be confirmed per restaurant certification.
